title: Crafting the Convincing Lure
description: Design the visual and psychological elements that trick users into clicking 'Enable Content'.

slides.title: The 'Enable Content' Problem 🟨
slides.content:
# Overcoming the Yellow Bar of Death

When a user opens a document with macros, they see:

> **SECURITY WARNING** Macros have been disabled. **[Enable Content]**

**The Challenge:** Convincing the user to click that button.

**Psychological Levers to Pull (Cialdini Review):**
- **Curiosity:** *"This content is protected. Enable macros to view."*
- **Urgency:** *"Invoice requires immediate approval. Enable macros to see details."*
- **Authority:** *"This document is from the IT Security team. Enable macros to verify compliance."*
- **Fear:** *"Your account has been flagged. Enable macros to review the security alert."*

**The Lure Content:** The visible part of the document (what they see **before** enabling macros) must **sell** the click.

quizData.question: A Word document opens and displays only a blurry image and the text: 'This document is protected. Enable macros to decrypt and view contents.' Which psychological trigger is primary?
quizData.explanation: Correct. The promise of hidden, protected information triggers intense curiosity. Users want to see what's 'inside'.

slides.title: Classic Lure Templates 📋
slides.content:
# Proven to Work

| Pretext | Document Type | Lure Content | Target |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Fake Invoice** | Word/Excel | Blurred invoice image. *"Enable macros to view invoice details."* | Accounts Payable |
| **Resume/CV** | Word | *"This document was created in an older version of Word. Enable macros for compatibility."* | HR, Recruiters |
| **Urgent Security Update** | Word | *"Your account requires verification. Enable macros to complete the process."* | General Staff |
| **Protected Report** | Excel | All cells hidden except one: *"This workbook is protected. Enable macros to unlock."* | Finance, Management |
| **Fax/Scanned Document** | Word | Blurry scanned image. *"Enable macros to improve image quality."* | Anyone |

slides.title: Excel Lure: Hiding All Sheets 📊
slides.content: You can hide all worksheets except a single 'lure' sheet that instructs the user to enable macros. When macros are enabled, the VBA code unhides the real content (or proceeds with the attack).
slides.codeSnippet:
Private Sub Workbook_Open()
    ' Unhide the real sheets
    Sheets("Confidential Data").Visible = xlSheetVisible
    Sheets("Lure").Visible = xlSheetHidden
    
    ' Then execute payload
    Call RunPayload
End Sub

slides.title: Word Lure: Blurry Images & Text 🖼️
slides.content:
# The 'Decryption' Trick

**Method:**
1.  Insert a **blurry screenshot** of a fake invoice or confidential report.
2.  Add a text box with the message: *"SECURE DOCUMENT: Content is encrypted. Enable Macros to decrypt and view."*
3.  Add a fake 'Microsoft Office' logo or security badge for authority.

**Why it works:**
- It mimics legitimate **Rights Management** or **Encryption** prompts that users have seen before.
- It creates a **puzzle** that the user wants to solve.
- The blurry image is **intriguing** but **unreadable**.

**VBA Component:** When macros are enabled, the VBA replaces the blurry image with a clear one (or just proceeds with the payload while showing a fake 'Decrypting...' progress bar).

quizData.question: An Excel file opens with all sheets hidden except one that says: 'This workbook is protected. Enable macros to view content.' This lure is effective because:

options.text: It triggers curiosity and mimics legitimate document protection features

quizData.explanation: Correct. Many legitimate business documents use workbook protection. Users are conditioned to click 'Enable' to access content.

slides.title: Filename and Subject Line Synergy 📨
slides.content:
# The One-Two Punch

**The Email Subject Line:**
- *"URGENT: Invoice #44532 - Action Required"*
- *"Resume: Senior Developer Candidate - John Smith"*
- *"Confidential: Q3 Earnings Report - Internal Only"*

**The Attachment Filename:**
- `Invoice_44532.docm` (`.docm` = Macro-Enabled Document)
- `John_Smith_Resume.docm`
- `Q3_Report_Confidential.xlsm` (`.xlsm` = Macro-Enabled Workbook)

**The Cognitive Alignment:**
The email creates **expectation**. The filename confirms it. The lure content reinforces it. The user's brain is primed to click **'Enable Content'** because they *want* to see the invoice/resume/report.

**Pro Tip:** Use **double extensions** sparingly. `Invoice.pdf.exe` is blocked. `Invoice_44532.docm` is a legitimate Office format.

slides.title: Adding Legitimacy with Metadata 📝
slides.content:
## The Devil is in the Details

Right-click the file -> Properties -> Details. Add plausible metadata:
- **Authors:** Match the sender name (e.g., "Accounts Payable Dept")
- **Company:** Target company name or your fake vendor name
- **Last Saved By:** Plausible username
- **Content Created:** A date within the last week

**Why?**
Some security tools and curious users check document properties. Blank or suspicious metadata is a red flag. Filling it out adds a layer of **Verisimilitude**.
